question: [File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_HASH] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted `transaction.hash` query parameter with a hash that collides with or is a prefix-truncated version of a real transaction hash, causing the mirror node to return or suppress a different transaction's data, leading to integrity confusion about which transaction was submitted?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_HASH] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `transaction.hash` value using the `HEX_PREFIX` ('0x') form versus the raw hex form to exploit inconsistent normalization in the lookup path, causing the same transaction to be found under one encoding but not the other, enabling selective suppression of transaction visibility?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_HASH, ETH_HASH_LENGTH] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `transaction.hash` value that is exactly `ETH_HASH_LENGTH` (32) bytes but belongs to a different chain or context, causing the mirror node to accept and return a cross-context transaction record as if it were a valid Hedera transaction, tampering with the apparent transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.NONCE] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly submit requests with different `nonce` values (0 through `MAX_INT32`) against a known transaction ID to enumerate which child transactions exist, leaking the internal nonce space of another user's scheduled or contract-triggered transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.NONCE, MAX_INT32] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `nonce` filter value equal to `MAX_INT32` (2147483647) to trigger an integer boundary condition in the SQL query parameter binding, causing an unexpected query result or error that reveals internal state about submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.NONCE] Can an unprivileged external user supply multiple repeated `nonce` query parameters (e.g., `nonce=1&nonce=2&nonce=3`) to exploit the 'honor the last' override behavior in `getTransactionByIdQueryParamConditions`, causing the final nonce value to silently replace earlier ones and return a different transaction than the one the caller intended to query, enabling transaction identity confusion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.SCHEDULED] Can an unprivileged external user toggle the `scheduled` filter between `true` and `false` on a known transaction ID to distinguish between a user-submitted transaction and its scheduled counterpart, leaking whether a transaction was submitted directly or via a schedule entity without any authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.SCHEDULED] Can an unprivileged external user submit repeated `scheduled` filter values (e.g., `scheduled=true&scheduled=false`) to exploit the last-value-wins behavior, causing the query to silently switch between scheduled and non-scheduled transaction views, leading to inconsistent or misleading transaction lookup results?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.NONCE, filterKeys.SCHEDULED] Can an unprivileged external user combine `nonce` and `scheduled` filters on a transaction ID lookup to enumerate the full matrix of child transactions spawned by a parent scheduled transaction, exposing the complete execution tree of another user's submitted transaction without authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TIMESTAMP] Can an unprivileged external user craft a `timestamp` filter using `NANOS_PER_SECOND` boundary values (e.g., exactly at second boundaries) to cause off-by-one errors in the consensus timestamp range query, causing a submitted transaction to appear absent or duplicated in the result set?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TIMESTAMP, SIXTY_SECONDS] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `timestamp` range spanning exactly `SIXTY_SECONDS` (60 seconds in nanoseconds) to exploit any window-based caching or pagination logic, causing transactions submitted near the boundary to be omitted from results, effectively hiding submitted transactions from observers?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TIMESTAMP, THIRTY_ONE_MINUTES] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `timestamp` range exceeding `THIRTY_ONE_MINUTES` to bypass any intended time-window restriction on transaction queries, retrieving a bulk history of submitted transactions that should be rate-limited or paginated?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TIMESTAMP, ONE_DAY_IN_NS] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `timestamp` filter with a value equal to `ONE_DAY_IN_NS` (86_400_000_000_000) to probe whether the system applies a maximum lookback window, and if not enforced, retrieve all transactions submitted within a full day in a single unbounded query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.RESULT, transactionResultFilter] Can an unprivileged external user filter transactions by `result=fail` using `transactionResultFilter.FAIL` to enumerate all failed transaction submissions by any account, leaking information about failed attempts that could reveal account activity patterns or smart contract revert conditions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.RESULT, transactionResultFilter] Can an unprivileged external user combine `result=success` with `account.id` filters to confirm whether a specific account's transaction was successfully submitted and executed, enabling targeted surveillance of another user's transaction outcomes without authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_TYPE] Can an unprivileged external user filter by specific `transactiontype` values (e.g., CRYPTOTRANSFER, CONTRACTCALL) to enumerate all transactions of a given type submitted by a target account, leaking the full financial activity profile of that account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_TYPE] Can an unprivileged external user submit an unrecognized or mixed-case `transactiontype` value to exploit case-insensitive or partial-match handling, causing the filter to match unintended transaction types and return transactions that should not be visible under the intended filter?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_INDEX, filterKeys.BLOCK_NUMBER] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `transaction.index` combined with a `block.number` to pinpoint the exact position of a submitted transaction within a block, enabling precise reconstruction of block contents and transaction ordering without any authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.TRANSACTION_INDEX, filterKeys.BLOCK_HASH] Can an unprivileged external user combine `transaction.index` with `block.hash` to retrieve a specific transaction by its block-level position, bypassing any account-level access controls and directly accessing any submitted transaction's data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.BLOCK_HASH, filterKeys.BLOCK_NUMBER] Can an unprivileged external user attempt to supply both `block.hash` and `block.number` simultaneously to trigger the `filterDependencyCheck` error path, causing a denial-of-service condition on the transaction query endpoint by flooding it with invalid combined filter requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: queryParamOperators] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `ne` (not-equal) operator for a `timestamp` filter to retrieve all transactions except those at a specific timestamp, effectively inverting the query and retrieving a much larger dataset than intended, causing resource exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: queryParamOperators] Can an unprivileged external user chain multiple `queryParamOperators` (e.g., `timestamp=gt:X&timestamp=lt:Y&timestamp=gte:Z`) to construct overlapping range conditions that confuse the query planner, causing incorrect transaction sets to be returned or performance degradation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: queryParamOperators.ne] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `ne` is excluded from `basicOperators` for certain fields but may still be accepted for others, submitting `ne` operators against `filterKeys.TIMESTAMP` to retrieve all transactions outside a specific timestamp, bypassing intended range restrictions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.LIMIT, MAX_INT32] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `limit` value equal to `MAX_INT32` (2147483647) to request an unbounded number of transaction records in a single response, causing memory exhaustion or denial of service on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: rest/constants.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Symbol: filterKeys.LIMIT] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `limit=0` or negative limit value to probe whether the validation correctly rejects non-positive limits, and if not, trigger an empty or undefined result set that masks submitted transactions from the response?
